Effect v4 Function: RcMap.make
episode_body:
Title: RcMap.make
Category: function
Module Path: effect/RcMap
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: models

Description: An `RcMap` can contain multiple reference counted resources that can be indexed by a key. The resources are lazily acquired on the first call to `get` and released when the last reference is released. Complex keys can extend `Equal` and `Hash` to allow lookups by value. **Options** - `capacity`: The maximum number of resources that can be held in the map. - `idleTimeToLive`: When the reference count reaches zero, the resource will be released after this duration.

Signature: make: { <K, A, E, R>(options: { readonly lookup: (key: K) => Effect.Effect<A, E, R> readonly idleTimeToLive?: Duration.DurationInput | ((key: K) => Duration.DurationInput) | undefined readonly capacity?: undefined

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, RcMap } from "effect"

Effect.gen(function*() {
  const map = yield* RcMap.make({
    lookup: (key: string) =>
      Effect.acquireRelease(
        Effect.succeed(`acquired ${key}`),
        () => Effect.log(`releasing ${key}`)
      )
  })

  // Get "foo" from the map twice, which will only acquire it once.
  // It will then be released once the scope closes.
  yield* RcMap.get(map, "foo").pipe(
    Effect.andThen(RcMap.get(map, "foo")),
    Effect.scoped
  )
})
```

Since: 3.5.0

name: Effect v4 Function: RcMap.get
episode_body:
Title: RcMap.get
Category: function
Module Path: effect/RcMap
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Retrieves a value from the RcMap by key. If the resource doesn't exist, it will be acquired using the lookup function. The resource is reference counted and will be released when the scope closes.

Signature: get: { <K>(key: K): <A, E>(self: RcMap<K, A, E>) => Effect.Effect<A, E, Scope.Scope> <K, A, E>(self: RcMap<K, A, E>, key: K): Effect.Effect<A, E, Scope.Scope> } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, RcMap } from "effect"

Effect.gen(function*() {
  const map = yield* RcMap.make({
    lookup: (key: string) =>
      Effect.acquireRelease(
        Effect.succeed(`Resource: ${key}`),
        () => Effect.log(`Released ${key}`)
      )
  })

  // Get a resource - it will be acquired on first access
  const resource = yield* RcMap.get(map, "database")
  console.log(resource) // "Resource: database"
}).pipe(Effect.scoped)
```

Since: 3.5.0

Signature: Filter<in Input, out Pass = Input, out Fail = Input, in Args extends ReadonlyArray<any> = []> { (input: Input, ...args: Args): Result.Result<Pass, Fail> }

Example:
```ts
import { Filter, Result } from "effect"

// A filter that only passes positive numbers
const positiveFilter: Filter.Filter<number> = (n) => n > 0 ? Result.succeed(n) : Result.fail(n)

console.log(positiveFilter(5)) // Result.succeed(5)
console.log(positiveFilter(-3)) // Result.fail(-3)
```

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Type: Filter.FilterEffect
episode_body:
Title: Filter.FilterEffect
Category: type
Module Path: effect/Filter
Export Kind: interface
Category Tag: Models

Description: Represents an effectful filter function that can produce Effects. Similar to a regular Filter, but the filtering operation itself can be effectful, allowing for asynchronous operations, error handling, and dependency injection.

Signature: FilterEffect< in Input, out Pass, out Fail, out E = never,

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, Filter, Result } from "effect"

// An effectful filter that validates user data
type User = { id: string; isActive: boolean }
type ValidationError = { message: string }

const validateUser: Filter.FilterEffect<
  string,
  User,
  User,
  ValidationError,
  never
> = (id) =>
  Effect.gen(function*() {
    const user: User = { id, isActive: id.length > 0 }
    return user.isActive ? Result.succeed(user) : Result.fail(user)
  })
```

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: Filter.make
episode_body:
Title: Filter.make
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Filter
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Constructors

Description: Creates a Filter from a function that returns either a `pass` or `fail` value. This is the primary constructor for creating custom filters. The function should return either `Result.succeed(value)` or `Result.fail(value)`.

Signature: make = <Input, Pass, Fail>( f: (input: Input) => Result.Result<Pass, Fail> ): Filter<Input, Pass, Fail> => f as any

Example:
```ts
import { Filter, Result } from "effect"

// Create a filter for positive numbers
const positiveFilter = Filter.make((n: number) => n > 0 ? Result.succeed(n) : Result.fail(n))

// Create a filter that transforms strings to uppercase
const uppercaseFilter = Filter.make((s: string) =>
  s.length > 0 ? Result.succeed(s.toUpperCase()) : Result.fail(s)
)
```

Since: 4.0.0

Title: Filter.fromPredicate
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Filter
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Constructors

Description: Creates a Filter that tries to apply a function and returns `fail` on error.

Signature: fromPredicate: { <A, B extends A>(refinement: Predicate.Refinement<A, B>): Filter<A, B, EqualsWith<A, B, A, Exclude<A, B>>> <A>(predicate: Predicate.Predicate<A>): Filter<A> } = <A, B extends A = A>(predicate: Predicate.Predicate<A> | Predicate.Refinement<A, B>): Filter<A, B> => (input: A) => predic

Example:
```ts
import { Filter, Result } from "effect"

// Create filter from predicate
const positiveNumbers = Filter.fromPredicate((n: number) => n > 0)
const nonEmptyStrings = Filter.fromPredicate((s: string) => s.length > 0)

// Type refinement
const isString = Filter.fromPredicate((x: unknown): x is string =>
  typeof x === "string"
)
```

name: Effect v4 Function: Filter.zip
episode_body:
Title: Filter.zip
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Filter
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Combinators

Description: Combines two filters into a tuple of their results. Both filters must succeed for the combination to succeed. If both pass, their outputs are combined into a tuple.

Signature: zip: { <InputR, PassR, FailR>( right: Filter<InputR, PassR, FailR> ): <InputL, PassL, FailL>( left: Filter<InputL, PassL, FailL>

Example:
```ts
import { Filter } from "effect"

const positiveNumbers = Filter.fromPredicate((n: number) => n > 0)
const evenNumbers = Filter.fromPredicate((n: number) => n % 2 === 0)

const positiveAndEven = Filter.zip(positiveNumbers, evenNumbers)
```

name: Effect v4 Function: FiberHandle.make
episode_body:
Title: FiberHandle.make
Category: function
Module Path: effect/FiberHandle
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: A FiberHandle can be used to store a single fiber. When the associated Scope is closed, the contained fiber will be interrupted. You can add a fiber to the handle using `FiberHandle.run`, and the fiber will be automatically removed from the FiberHandle when it completes.

Signature: make = <A = unknown, E = unknown>(): Effect.Effect<FiberHandle<A, E>, never, Scope.Scope> => Effect.acquireRelease( Effect.sync(() => makeUnsafe<A, E>()), (handle) => { const state = handle.state

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, FiberHandle } from "effect"

Effect.gen(function*() {
  const handle = yield* FiberHandle.make()

  // run some effects
  yield* FiberHandle.run(handle, Effect.never)
  // this will interrupt the previous fiber
  yield* FiberHandle.run(handle, Effect.never)

  yield* Effect.sleep(1000)
}).pipe(
  Effect.scoped // The fiber will be interrupted when the scope is closed
)
```

episode_body:
Title: FiberHandle.join
Category: function
Module Path: effect/FiberHandle
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: If any of the Fiber's in the handle terminate with a failure, the returned Effect will terminate with the first failure that occurred.

Signature: join = <A, E>(self: FiberHandle<A, E>): Effect.Effect<void, E> => Deferred.await(self.deferred as Deferred.Deferred<void, E>)

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, FiberHandle } from "effect"

Effect.gen(function*() {
  const handle = yield* FiberHandle.make()
  yield* FiberHandle.set(handle, Effect.runFork(Effect.fail("error")))

  // parent fiber will fail with "error"
  yield* FiberHandle.join(handle)
})
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: FiberHandle.awaitEmpty
episode_body:
Title: FiberHandle.awaitEmpty
Category: function
Module Path: effect/FiberHandle
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Wait for the fiber in the FiberHandle to complete.

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, FiberHandle } from "effect"

Effect.gen(function*() {
  const handle = yield* FiberHandle.make()

  // Start a long-running effect
  yield* FiberHandle.run(handle, Effect.sleep(1000))

  // Wait for the fiber to complete
  yield* FiberHandle.awaitEmpty(handle)

  console.log("Fiber completed")
})
```

Since: 3.13.0

```ts

import * as assert from "node:assert"

  minimum: BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("1"),
  maximum: BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("5")
})

assert.deepStrictEqual(
  clamp(BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("3")),
  BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("3")
)
assert.deepStrictEqual(
  clamp(BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("0")),
  BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("1")
)
assert.deepStrictEqual(
  clamp(BigDecimal.fromStringUnsafe("6")),
  BigDecimal.fromS

name: Effect v4 Function: BigDecimal.Equivalence
episode_body:
Title: BigDecimal.Equivalence
Category: function
Module Path: effect/BigDecimal
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: instances

Description: Provides an `Equivalence` instance for `BigDecimal` that determines equality between BigDecimal values.

Signature: Equivalence: Equ.Equivalence<BigDecimal> = Equ.make((self, that) => { if (self.scale > that.scale) { return scale(that, self.scale).value === self.value }

Example:
```ts
import { BigDecimal } from "effect"

const a = BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe(1.50)
const b = BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe(1.5)
const c = BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe(2.0)

console.log(BigDecimal.Equivalence(a, b)) // true (1.50 === 1.5)
console.log(BigDecimal.Equivalence(a, c)) // false (1.50 !== 2.0)
```

Example:
```ts
import { BigDecimal } from "effect"

const a = BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe(1.5)
const b = BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe(1.50)
const c = BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe(2.0)

console.log(BigDecimal.equals(a, b)) // true
console.log(BigDecimal.equals(a, c)) // false
```

name: Effect v4 Function: BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe

Title: BigDecimal.fromNumberUnsafe
Category: function
Module Path: effect/BigDecimal
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: Creates a `BigDecimal` from a `number` value. It is not recommended to convert a floating point number to a decimal directly, as the floating point representation may be unexpected. Throws a `RangeError` if the number is not finite (`NaN`, `+Infinity` or `-Infinity`).

Signature: fromNumberUnsafe = (n: number): BigDecimal => { const out = fromNumber(n) if (out) return out throw new RangeError(`Number must be finite, got ${n}`) }

Example:
```ts
import { fromNumberUnsafe, make } from "effect/BigDecimal"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(fromNumberUnsafe(123), make(123n, 0))
assert.deepStrictEqual(fromNumberUnsafe(123.456), make(123456n, 3))
```

name: Effect v4 Function: BigDecimal.fromNumber
episode_body:
Title: BigDecimal.fromNumber
Category: function
Module Path: effect/BigDecimal
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: Creates a `BigDecimal` from a `number` value. It is not recommended to convert a floating point number to a decimal directly, as the floating point representation may be unexpected. Returns `undefined` for `NaN`, `+Infinity` or `-Infinity`.

Signature: fromNumber = (n: number): BigDecimal | undefined => { if (!Number.isFinite(n)) { return undefined }

Example:
```ts
import { BigDecimal } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(BigDecimal.fromNumber(123), BigDecimal.make(123n, 0))
assert.deepStrictEqual(
  BigDecimal.fromNumber(123.456),
  BigDecimal.make(123456n, 3)
)
assert.deepStrictEqual(BigDecimal.fromNumber(Infinity), undefined)
```

```

name: Effect v4 Function: Deferred.make
episode_body:
Title: Deferred.make
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Deferred
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: Creates a new `Deferred`.

Signature: make = <A, E = never>(): Effect<Deferred<A, E>> => internalEffect.sync(() => makeUnsafe())

Example:
```ts
import { Deferred, Effect } from "effect"

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  const deferred = yield* Deferred.make<number>()
  yield* Deferred.succeed(deferred, 42)
  const value = yield* Deferred.await(deferred)
  console.log(value) // 42
})
```

      // Acquire permit for the duration of this scope
      yield* TxSemaphore.withPermitScoped(semaphore)

name: Effect v4 Function: O.none
episode_body:
Title: O.none
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Constructors

Description: Creates an `Option` representing the absence of a value. **When to use** - Representing a missing or uninitialized value - Returning "no result" from a function **Behavior** - Returns `Option<never>`, which is a subtype of `Option<A>` for any `A` - Always returns the same singleton instance

Signature: none = <A = never>(): Option<A> => option.none

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

//      ┌─── Option<never>
//      ▼
const noValue = O.none()

console.log(noValue)
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.some
episode_body:
Title: O.some
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Constructors

Description: Wraps the given value into an `Option` to represent its presence. **When to use** - Wrapping a known-present value as `Option` - Returning a successful result from a partial function **Behavior** - Always returns `Some<A>` - Does not filter `null` or `undefined`; use fromNullishOr for that

Signature: some: <A>(value: A) => Option<A> = option.some

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

//      ┌─── Option<number>
//      ▼
const value = O.some(1)

console.log(value)
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 1 }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.isOption
episode_body:
Title: O.isOption
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Guards

Description: Determines whether the given value is an `Option`. **When to use** - Validating unknown values at runtime boundaries - Type-narrowing in union types **Behavior** - Returns `true` for both `Some` and `None` instances - Acts as a type guard, narrowing the input to `Option<unknown>`

Signature: isOption: (input: unknown) => input is Option<unknown> = option.isOption

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.isOption(O.some(1)))
// Output: true

console.log(O.isOption(O.none()))
// Output: true

console.log(O.isOption({}))
// Output: false
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.isNone
episode_body:
Title: O.isNone
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Guards

Description: Checks whether an `Option` is `None` (absent). **When to use** - Branching on absence before accessing `.value` **Behavior** - Acts as a type guard, narrowing to `None<A>`

Signature: isNone: <A>(self: Option<A>) => self is None<A> = option.isNone

```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

// Output: false

console.log(O.isNone(O.none()))

Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Guards

Description: Checks whether an `Option` contains a value (`Some`). **When to use** - Branching on presence before accessing `.value` **Behavior** - Acts as a type guard, narrowing to `Some<A>`

Signature: isSome: <A>(self: Option<A>) => self is Some<A> = option.isSome

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

name: Effect v4 Function: O.match
episode_body:
Title: O.match
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Pattern matching

Description: Pattern-matches on an `Option`, handling both `None` and `Some` cases. **When to use** - Exhaustively handling both branches in one expression - Transforming an `Option` into a plain value **Behavior** - If `None`, calls `onNone` and returns its result - If `Some`, calls `onSome` with the value and returns its result - Supports the `dual` API (data-last and data-first)

Signature: match: { <B, A, C = B>(options: { readonly onNone: LazyArg<B> readonly onSome: (a: A) => C }): (self: Option<A>) => B | C

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

const message = O.match(O.some(1), {
  onNone: () => "Option is empty",
  onSome: (value) => `Option has a value: ${value}`
})

console.log(message)
// Output: "Option has a value: 1"
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.toRefinement
episode_body:
Title: O.toRefinement
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Conversions

Description: Converts an `Option`-returning function into a type guard (refinement). **When to use** - Turning a parsing function into a type-narrowing predicate - Filtering arrays with `Array.prototype.filter` **Behavior** - Returns `true` when the original function returns `Some` - Returns `false` when the original function returns `None` - Narrows the input type to `B` on success

Signature: toRefinement = <A, B extends A>(f: (a: A) => Option<B>): (a: A) => a is B => (a: A): a is B => isSome(f(a))

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

type MyData = string | number

const parseString = (data: MyData): O.Option<string> =>
  typeof data === "string" ? O.some(data) : O.none()

//      ┌─── (a: MyData) => a is string
//      ▼
const isString = O.toRefinement(parseString)

console.log(isString("a"))
// Output: true

console.log(isString(1))
// Output: false
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.fromIterable
episode_body:
Title: O.fromIterable
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Constructors

Description: Wraps the first element of an `Iterable` in a `Some`, or returns `None` if the iterable is empty. **When to use** - Safely extracting the head of a collection - Working with generators or lazy iterables **Behavior** - Only consumes the first element; does not iterate the rest - Returns `None` for empty iterables

Signature: fromIterable = <A>(collection: Iterable<A>): Option<A> => { for (const a of collection) { return some(a) } return none()

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.fromIterable([1, 2, 3]))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 1 }

console.log(O.fromIterable([]))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.getSuccess
episode_body:
Title: O.getSuccess
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Conversions

Description: Converts a `Result` into an `Option`, keeping only the success value. **When to use** - Discarding the error channel when you only care about success **Behavior** - `Ok` → `Some` with the success value - `Err` → `None` (error is discarded)

Signature: getSuccess: <A, E>(self: Result<A, E>) => Option<A> = result.getSuccess

Example:
```ts
import { Option, Result } from "effect"

console.log(O.getSuccess(Result.succeed("ok")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'ok' }

console.log(O.getSuccess(Result.fail("err")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.getOrElse
episode_body:
Title: O.getOrElse
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Getters

Description: Extracts the value from a `Some`, or evaluates a fallback thunk on `None`. **When to use** - Providing a default value for an absent `Option` - Unwrapping with lazy evaluation of the fallback **Behavior** - `Some` → returns the inner value - `None` → calls `onNone()` and returns its result - `onNone` is only called when needed (lazy)

Signature: getOrElse: { <B>(onNone: LazyArg<B>): <A>(self: Option<A>) => B | A <A, B>(self: Option<A>, onNone: LazyArg<B>): A | B } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.some(1).pipe(O.getOrElse(() => 0)))
// Output: 1

console.log(O.none().pipe(O.getOrElse(() => 0)))
// Output: 0
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.orElse
episode_body:
Title: O.orElse
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Error handling

Description: Returns the fallback `Option` if `self` is `None`; otherwise returns `self`. **When to use** - Chaining fallback `Option` computations - Building priority chains of optional values **Behavior** - `Some` → returns `self` unchanged - `None` → evaluates and returns `that()` - `that` is lazily evaluated

Signature: orElse: { <B>(that: LazyArg<Option<B>>): <A>(self: Option<A>) => Option<B | A> <A, B>(self: Option<A>, that: LazyArg<Option<B>>): Option<A | B> } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.none().pipe(O.orElse(() => O.some("b"))))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'b' }

console.log(O.some("a").pipe(O.orElse(() => O.some("b"))))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'a' }
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.orElseSome
episode_body:
Title: O.orElseSome
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Error handling

Description: Returns `Some` of the fallback value if `self` is `None`; otherwise returns `self`. **When to use** - Providing a default plain value (not an `Option`) as fallback **Behavior** - `Some` → returns `self` unchanged - `None` → calls `onNone()`, wraps result in `Some`, and returns it

Signature: orElseSome: { <B>(onNone: LazyArg<B>): <A>(self: Option<A>) => Option<B | A> <A, B>(self: Option<A>, onNone: LazyArg<B>): Option<A | B> } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.none().pipe(O.orElseSome(() => "b")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'b' }

console.log(O.some("a").pipe(O.orElseSome(() => "b")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'a' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.orElseResult
episode_body:
Title: O.orElseResult
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Error handling

Description: Like orElse, but wraps the result in a `Result` to indicate the source of the value. **When to use** - Distinguishing whether a value came from the primary or fallback `Option` **Behavior** - `self` is `Some` → `Some(Result.fail(value))` (value from primary) - `self` is `None`, `that()` is `Some` → `Some(Result.succeed(value))` (value from fallback) - Both `None` → `None`

Signature: orElseResult: { <B>(that: LazyArg<Option<B>>): <A>(self: Option<A>) => Option<Result<B, A>> <A, B>(self: Option<A>, that: LazyArg<Option<B>>): Option<Result<B, A>> } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.orElseResult(O.some("primary"), () => O.some("fallback")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: { _tag: 'Failure', value: 'primary' } }

console.log(O.orElseResult(O.none(), () => O.some("fallback")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: { _tag: 'Success', value: 'fallback' } }
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.firstSomeOf
episode_body:
Title: O.firstSomeOf
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Error handling

Description: Returns the first `Some` found in an iterable of `Option`s, or `None` if all are `None`. **When to use** - Searching for the first available value in a priority list **Behavior** - Short-circuits on the first `Some` - Returns `None` only when every element is `None`

Signature: firstSomeOf = <T, C extends Iterable<Option<T>> = Iterable<Option<T>>>( collection: C ): [C] extends [Iterable<Option<infer A>>] ? Option<A> : never => { let out: Option<unknown> = none() for (out of collection) {

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.firstSomeOf([
  O.none(),
  O.some(1),
  O.some(2)
]))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 1 }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.map
episode_body:
Title: O.map
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Mapping

Description: Transforms the value inside a `Some` using the provided function, leaving `None` unchanged. **When to use** - Applying a pure transformation to an optional value - Chaining transformations in a pipeline **Behavior** - `Some` → applies `f` and wraps the result in a new `Some` - `None` → returns `None` unchanged - Does not mutate the input

Signature: map: { <A, B>(f: (a: A) => B): (self: Option<A>) => Option<B> <A, B>(self: Option<A>, f: (a: A) => B): Option<B> } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.map(O.some(2), (n) => n * 2))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 4 }

console.log(O.map(O.none(), (n: number) => n * 2))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.as
episode_body:
Title: O.as
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Mapping

Description: Replaces the value inside a `Some` with a constant, leaving `None` unchanged. **When to use** - Preserving presence/absence while discarding the original value **Behavior** - `Some` → `Some(b)` - `None` → `None`

Signature: as: { <B>(b: B): <X>(self: Option<X>) => Option<B> <X, B>(self: Option<X>, b: B): Option<B> } = dual(2, <X, B>(self: Option<X>, b: B): Option<B> => map(self, () => b))

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.as(O.some(42), "new value"))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'new value' }

console.log(O.as(O.none(), "new value"))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

Signature: flatten: <A>(self: Option<Option<A>>) => Option<A> = flatMap(identity)

Title: O.zipRight
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Zipping

Description: Sequences two `Option`s, keeping the value from the second if both are `Some`. **When to use** - Running a side-condition that must succeed, then using the second value **Behavior** - Both `Some` → returns `that` - Either `None` → returns `None`

Signature: zipRight: { <B>(that: Option<B>): <_>(self: Option<_>) => Option<B> <X, B>(self: Option<X>, that: Option<B>): Option<B> } = dual(2, <X, B>(self: Option<X>, that: Option<B>): Option<B> => flatMap(self, () => that))

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.zipRight(O.some(1), O.some("hello")))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 'hello' }

console.log(O.zipRight(O.none(), O.some("hello")))

Signature: zipLeft: { <_>(that: Option<_>): <A>(self: Option<A>) => Option<A> <A, X>(self: Option<A>, that: Option<X>): Option<A> } = dual(2, <A, X>(self: Option<A>, that: Option<X>): Option<A> => tap(self, () => that))

import { Option } from "effect"

Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Sequencing

Description: Composes two `Option`-returning functions into a single function that chains them together. **When to use** - Building pipelines of partial functions (Kleisli composition) **Behavior** - Calls `afb(a)`, then if `Some`, calls `bfc` with its value - Short-circuits to `None` if either function returns `None`

Signature: composeK: { <B, C>(bfc: (b: B) => Option<C>): <A>(afb: (a: A) => Option<B>) => (a: A) => Option<C> <A, B, C>(afb: (a: A) => Option<B>, bfc: (b: B) => Option<C>): (a: A) => Option<C> } = dual(2, <A, B, C>(afb: (a: A) => Option<B>, bfc: (b: B) => Option<C>) => (a: A): Option<C> => flatMap(afb(a), bfc)

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

const parse = (s: string): O.Option<number> =>
  isNaN(Number(s)) ? O.none() : O.some(Number(s))

const double = (n: number): O.Option<number> =>
  n > 0 ? O.some(n * 2) : O.none()

const parseAndDouble = O.composeK(parse, double)

console.log(parseAndDouble("42"))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 84 }

console.log(parseAndDouble("not a number"))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.tap
episode_body:
Title: O.tap
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Sequencing

Description: Runs a side-effecting `Option`-returning function on the value of a `Some`, returning the original `Option` if the function returns `Some`, or `None` if it returns `None`. **When to use** - Validating a value without transforming it - Adding a side-condition check in a pipeline **Behavior** - `None` → `None` - `Some` → calls `f(value)`; if result is `Some`, returns original `self`; if `None`, returns `None`

Signature: tap: { <A, X>(f: (a: A) => Option<X>): (self: Option<A>) => Option<A> <A, X>(self: Option<A>, f: (a: A) => Option<X>): Option<A> } = dual(2, <A, X>(self: Option<A>, f: (a: A) => Option<X>): Option<A> => flatMap(self, (a) => map(f(a), () => a)))

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

const getInteger = (n: number) =>
  Number.isInteger(n) ? O.some(n) : O.none()

console.log(O.tap(O.some(1), getInteger))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 1 }

console.log(O.tap(O.some(1.14), getInteger))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.product
episode_body:
Title: O.product
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Combining

Description: Combines two `Option`s into a `Some` containing a tuple `[A, B]` if both are `Some`. **When to use** - Pairing two optional values together **Behavior** - Both `Some` → `Some([a, b])` - Either `None` → `None`

Signature: product = <A, B>(self: Option<A>, that: Option<B>): Option<[A, B]> => isSome(self) && isSome(that) ? some([self.value, that.value]) : none()

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.product(O.some("hello"), O.some(42)))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: ['hello', 42] }

console.log(O.product(O.none(), O.some(42)))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

Title: O.reduceCompact
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Reducing

Description: Reduces an iterable of `Option`s to a single value, skipping `None` entries. **When to use** - Aggregating values from a collection where some may be absent **Behavior** - Iterates through the collection, applying `f` only to `Some` values - `None` values are skipped entirely - Returns the accumulated result

Signature: reduceCompact: { <B, A>(b: B, f: (b: B, a: A) => B): (self: Iterable<Option<A>>) => B <A, B>(self: Iterable<Option<A>>, b: B, f: (b: B, a: A) => B): B } = dual( 3,

Example:
```ts
import { Option, pipe } from "effect"

const items = [O.some(1), O.none(), O.some(2), O.none()]

console.log(pipe(items, O.reduceCompact(0, (b, a) => b + a)))
// Output: 3
```

Signature: toArray = <A>(self: Option<A>): Array<A> => isNone(self) ? [] : [self.value]

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

Effect v4 Function: O.makeEquivalence
episode_body:
Title: O.makeEquivalence
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Equivalence

Description: Creates an `Equivalence` for `Option<A>` from an `Equivalence` for `A`. **When to use** - Comparing two `Option` values for structural equality **Behavior** - `None` vs `None` → `true` - `Some` vs `None` (or vice versa) → `false` - `Some(a)` vs `Some(b)` → delegates to the provided `Equivalence`

Signature: makeEquivalence = <A>(isEquivalent: Equivalence.Equivalence<A>): Equivalence.Equivalence<Option<A>> => Equivalence.make((x, y) => isNone(x) ? isNone(y) : isNone(y) ? false : isEquivalent(x.value, y.value))

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence, Option } from "effect"

const eq = O.makeEquivalence(Equivalence.strictEqual<number>())

console.log(eq(O.some(1), O.some(1)))
// Output: true

console.log(eq(O.some(1), O.some(2)))
// Output: false

console.log(eq(O.none(), O.none()))
// Output: true
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.makeOrder
episode_body:
Title: O.makeOrder
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Sorting

Description: Creates an `Order` for `Option<A>` from an `Order` for `A`. **When to use** - Sorting collections of `Option` values **Behavior** - `None` is considered less than any `Some` - Two `Some` values are compared using the provided `Order` - Two `None` values are equal (returns `0`)

Signature: makeOrder = <A>(O: Order<A>): Order<Option<A>> => order.make((self, that) => isSome(self) ? (isSome(that) ? O(self.value, that.value) : 1) : -1)

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"
import * as N from "effect/Number"

const ord = O.makeOrder(N.Order)

console.log(ord(O.none(), O.some(1)))
// Output: -1

console.log(ord(O.some(1), O.none()))
// Output: 1

console.log(ord(O.some(1), O.some(2)))
// Output: -1
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.lift2
episode_body:
Title: O.lift2
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Lifting

Description: Lifts a binary function to operate on two `Option` values. **When to use** - Reusing an existing binary function in an `Option` context **Behavior** - Both `Some` → applies `f` and wraps in `Some` - Either `None` → `None`

Signature: lift2 = <A, B, C>(f: (a: A, b: B) => C): { (that: Option<B>): (self: Option<A>) => Option<C> (self: Option<A>, that: Option<B>): Option<C> } => dual(2, (self: Option<A>, that: Option<B>): Option<C> => zipWith(self, that, f))

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

const addOptions = O.lift2((a: number, b: number) => a + b)

console.log(addOptions(O.some(2), O.some(3)))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 5 }

console.log(addOptions(O.some(2), O.none()))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.liftPredicate
episode_body:
Title: O.liftPredicate
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Lifting

Description: Lifts a `Predicate` or `Refinement` into the `Option` context: returns `Some(value)` when the predicate holds, `None` otherwise. **When to use** - Converting a boolean check into an `Option`-returning function - Validating input and wrapping it in `Option` **Behavior** - `predicate(value)` is `true` → `Some(value)` - `predicate(value)` is `false` → `None` - Supports refinements for type narrowing

Signature: liftPredicate: { // Note: I intentionally avoid using the NoInfer pattern here. <A, B extends A>(refinement: Refinement<A, B>): (a: A) => Option<B> <B extends A, A = B>(predicate: Predicate<A>): (b: B) => Option<B> <A, B extends A>( self: A,

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

const parsePositive = O.liftPredicate((n: number) => n > 0)

console.log(parsePositive(1))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 1 }

console.log(parsePositive(-1))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.containsWith
episode_body:
Title: O.containsWith
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Elements

Description: Checks if an `Option` contains a value equivalent to the given one, using a custom `Equivalence`. **When to use** - Testing membership with a custom equality check **Behavior** - `Some` where `isEquivalent(value, a)` is `true` → `true` - `Some` where not equivalent, or `None` → `false`

Signature: containsWith = <A>(isEquivalent: (self: A, that: A) => boolean): { (a: A): (self: Option<A>) => boolean (self: Option<A>, a: A): boolean } => dual(2, (self: Option<A>, a: A): boolean => isNone(self) ? false : isEquivalent(self.value, a))

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence, Option } from "effect"

const check = O.containsWith(Equivalence.strictEqual<number>())

console.log(O.some(2).pipe(check(2)))
// Output: true

console.log(O.some(1).pipe(check(2)))
// Output: false

console.log(O.none().pipe(check(2)))
// Output: false
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.contains
episode_body:
Title: O.contains
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Elements

Description: Checks if an `Option` contains a value equal to the given one, using default structural equality. **When to use** - Quick membership test with standard equality **Behavior** - `Some` where `Equal.equals(value, a)` is `true` → `true` - `Some` where not equal, or `None` → `false`

Signature: contains: { <A>(a: A): (self: Option<A>) => boolean <A>(self: Option<A>, a: A): boolean } = containsWith(Equal.asEquivalence())

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

console.log(O.some(2).pipe(O.contains(2)))
// Output: true

console.log(O.some(1).pipe(O.contains(2)))
// Output: false

console.log(O.none().pipe(O.contains(2)))
// Output: false
```

Title: O.exists
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Elements

Description: Tests if the value in a `Some` satisfies a predicate or refinement. **When to use** - Checking a condition on an optional value without unwrapping **Behavior** - `None` → `false` - `Some` where `predicate(value)` is `true` → `true` - `Some` where `predicate(value)` is `false` → `false` - With a refinement, narrows the `Option` type on `true`

Signature: exists: { <A, B extends A>(refinement: Refinement<NoInfer<A>, B>): (self: Option<A>) => self is Option<B> <A>(predicate: Predicate<NoInfer<A>>): (self: Option<A>) => boolean <A, B extends A>(self: Option<A>, refinement: Refinement<A, B>): self is Option<B> <A>(self: Option<A>, predicate: Predicate<A

Example:
```ts
import { Option } from "effect"

const isEven = (n: number) => n % 2 === 0

console.log(O.some(2).pipe(O.exists(isEven)))
// Output: true

console.log(O.some(1).pipe(O.exists(isEven)))
// Output: false

console.log(O.none().pipe(O.exists(isEven)))
// Output: false
```

name: Effect v4 Function: O.bindTo
episode_body:
Title: O.bindTo
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Do notation

Description: Gives a name to the value of an `Option`, creating a single-key record inside `Some`. Starting point for the do notation pipeline. **When to use** - Beginning a do notation chain by naming the first value

Signature: bindTo: { <N extends string>(name: N): <A>(self: Option<A>) => Option<{ [K in N]: A }> <A, N extends string>(self: Option<A>, name: N): Option<{ [K in N]: A }> } = doNotation.bindTo<OptionTypeLambda>(map)

Example:
```ts
import { Option, pipe } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const result = pipe(
  O.some(2),
  O.bindTo("x"),
  O.bind("y", () => O.some(3)),
  O.let("sum", ({ x, y }) => x + y)
)
assert.deepStrictEqual(result, O.some({ x: 2, y: 3, sum: 5 }))
```

Signature: bind: { <N extends string, A extends object, B>( name: Exclude<N, keyof A>, f: (a: NoInfer<A>) => Option<B> ): (self: Option<A>) => Option<{ [K in N | keyof A]: K extends keyof A ? A[K] : B }>

Example:

import { Option, pipe } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const result = pipe(
  O.Do,

)

Category: function

Description: An `Option` containing an empty record `{}`, used as the starting point for do notation chains. **When to use** - Starting a do notation pipeline before adding bindings

Signature: Do: Option<{}> = some({})

Example:
```ts
import { Option, pipe } from "effect"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const result = pipe(
  O.Do,
  O.bind("x", () => O.some(2)),
  O.bind("y", () => O.some(3)),
  O.let("sum", ({ x, y }) => x + y),
  O.filter(({ x, y }) => x * y > 5)
)
assert.deepStrictEqual(result, O.some({ x: 2, y: 3, sum: 5 }))

Effect v4 Function: O.makeReducer
episode_body:
Title: O.makeReducer
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: uncategorized

Description: Creates a `Reducer` for `Option<A>` that prioritizes the first non-`None` value and combines values when both are `Some`. **When to use** - Building a reducer that falls back to the first available value - Combining optional values where either side may be absent **Behavior** - `None` + `None` → `None` - `Some(a)` + `None` → `Some(a)` - `None` + `Some(b)` → `Some(b)` - `Some(a)` + `Some(b)` → `Some(combine(a, b))` - Initial value is `None`

Signature: makeReducer<A>(combiner: Combiner.Combiner<A>): Reducer.Reducer<Option<A>> { return Reducer.make((self, that) => { if (isNone(self)) return that if (isNone(that)) return self return some(combiner.combine(self.value, that.value))

Example:
```ts
import { Number, Option } from "effect"

const reducer = O.makeReducer(Number.ReducerSum)
console.log(reducer.combineAll([O.some(1), O.none(), O.some(2)]))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 3 }
```

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: O.makeCombinerFailFast
episode_body:
Title: O.makeCombinerFailFast
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Option
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: uncategorized

Description: Creates a `Combiner` for `Option<A>` with fail-fast semantics: returns `None` if either operand is `None`. **When to use** - Operations that require both values to be present **Behavior** - `None` + anything → `None` - anything + `None` → `None` - `Some(a)` + `Some(b)` → `Some(combine(a, b))`

Signature: makeCombinerFailFast<A>(combiner: Combiner.Combiner<A>): Combiner.Combiner<Option<A>> { return Combiner.make((self, that) => { if (isNone(self) || isNone(that)) return none() return some(combiner.combine(self.value, that.value)) })

Example:
```ts
import { Number, Option } from "effect"

const combiner = O.makeCombinerFailFast(Number.ReducerSum)
console.log(combiner.combine(O.some(1), O.some(2)))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'Some', value: 3 }

console.log(combiner.combine(O.some(1), O.none()))
// Output: { _id: 'Option', _tag: 'None' }
```

Description: A namespace providing utilities for TaggedEnum types. This namespace contains types and utilities for working with tagged enums, which are discriminated unions with structural equality semantics.

import { Data } from "effect"

// Basic tagged enum usage
const { Failure, Success } = Data.taggedEnum<
  | { readonly _tag: "Success"; readonly value: number }
  | { readonly _tag: "Failure"; readonly error: string }
>()

const result = Success({ value: 42 })
console.log(result._tag) // "Success"
console.log(result.value) // 42
```

Title: Data.Error
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Data
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: Create a structured error constructor that supports Effect's error handling. This constructor creates errors that are both `Cause.YieldableError` (can be yielded in Effect generators) and have structural equality semantics.

Signature: Error: new<A extends Record<string, any> = {}>( args: Types.Equals<A, {}> extends true ? void : { readonly [P in keyof A]: A[P] } ) => Cause.YieldableError & Readonly<A> = core.Error

Example:
```ts
import { Data, Effect } from "effect"

class NetworkError extends Data.Error<{ code: number; message: string }> {}

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  yield* new NetworkError({ code: 500, message: "Server error" })
})

Effect.runSync(Effect.exit(program))
// Exit.fail(NetworkError({ code: 500, message: "Server error" }))
```

Title: Schedule.InputMetadata

Description: Metadata provided to schedule functions containing timing and input information.

Signature: InputMetadata<Input> { readonly input: Input readonly attempt: number readonly start: number readonly now: number

Example:
```ts
import { Console, Effect, Schedule } from "effect"

// Custom schedule that uses input metadata
const metadataAwareSchedule = Schedule.spaced("1 second").pipe(
  Schedule.collectWhile((metadata) =>
    Effect.succeed(metadata.attempt <= 5 && metadata.elapsed < 10000)
  )
)

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  yield* Effect.repeat(
    Console.log("Task execution"),
    metadataAwareSchedule
  )
})
```

Title: Schedule.eitherWith
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Schedule
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: utilities

Description: Combines two `Schedule`s by recurring if either of the two schedules wants to recur, using the minimum of the two durations between recurrences and outputting the result of the combination of both schedule outputs using the specified `combine` function.

Signature: eitherWith: { <Output2, Input2, Error2, Env2, Output, Output3>( other: Schedule<Output2, Input2, Error2, Env2>, combine: (selfOutput: Output, otherOutput: Output2) => Output3 ): <Input, Error, Env>(

Example:
```ts
import { Console, Effect, Schedule } from "effect"

// Combine schedules with either semantics and custom combination
const primarySchedule = Schedule.exponential("100 millis").pipe(
  Schedule.map(() => Effect.succeed("primary")),
  Schedule.take(2)
)
const fallbackSchedule = Schedule.spaced("500 millis").pipe(
  Schedule.map(() => Effect.succeed("fallback"))
)

const combined = Schedule.eitherWith(
  primarySchedule,
  fallbackSchedule,
  (primary, fallback) => `${primary}+${fallback}`
)

Title: Schedule.elapsed
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Schedule
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: A schedule that always recurs and returns the total elapsed duration since the first recurrence. This schedule never stops and outputs the cumulative time that has passed since the schedule started executing. Useful for tracking execution time or implementing time-based logic.

Signature: elapsed: Schedule<Duration.Duration> = fromStepWithMetadata( effect.succeed((meta) => effect.succeed([Duration.millis(meta.elapsed), Duration.zero] as const)) )

Example:
```ts
import { Console, Duration, Effect, Schedule } from "effect"

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  yield* Effect.repeat(
    Console.log("Running task..."),
    Schedule.spaced("1 second").pipe(
      Schedule.both(Schedule.elapsed),
      Schedule.tapOutput(([count, duration]) =>
        Console.log(`Run ${count}, elapsed: ${Duration.toMillis(duration)}ms`)
      ),
      Schedule.take(5)
    )
  )
})
```

Title: Schedule.take

```ts
import { Console, Effect, Schedule } from "effect"

// Limit an infinite schedule to run only 5 times
const limitedHeartbeat = Schedule.spaced("1 second").pipe(
  Schedule.take(5) // Will stop after 5 executions
)

const heartbeatProgram = Effect.gen(function*() {
  yield* Effect.repeat(
    Effect.gen(function*() {
      yield* Console.log(`Heartbeat at ${new Date().toISOString()}`)
      return "pulse"
    }),
    limitedHeartbeat
  )

  yield* Console.log("Heartbeat sequence completed")

Category: function

  // This would typically be used in enterprise/cloud scenarios
  yield* Console.log(`Processing ${massiveDataset} bytes of data`)

  // Such large files would require specialized streaming
  const stream = fs.stream("massive-dataset.bin", {
    chunkS

episode_body:
Title: Config.map
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Config
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Mapping

Description: Transforms the parsed value of a config with a pure function. When to use: - Post-processing a config value (e.g. trimming, uppercasing, wrapping). - The transformation cannot fail. Use mapOrFail if it can. Supports both data-last and data-first calling conventions.

Signature: map: { <A, B>(f: (a: A) => B): (self: Config<A>) => Config<B> <A, B>(self: Config<A>, f: (a: A) => B): Config<B> } = dual(2, <A, B>(self: Config<A>, f: (a: A) => B): Config<B> => { return make((provider) => Effect.map(self.parse(provider), f))

Example:
```ts
import { Config, ConfigProvider, Effect } from "effect"

const upper = Config.string("name").pipe(
  Config.map((s) => s.toUpperCase())
)

const provider = ConfigProvider.fromUnknown({ name: "alice" })
// Effect.runSync(upper.parse(provider)) // "ALICE"
```

Supports both data-last and data-first calling conventions.

Example:
```ts
import { Config } from "effect"

const hostConfig = Config.string("HOST").pipe(
  Config.orElse(() => Config.succeed("localhost"))
)
```

The default is lazily evaluated. Supports both data-last and data-first calling conventions.

import { Config, ConfigProvider, Effect } from "effect"

const port = Config.number("port").pipe(Config.withDefault(() => 3000))

const provider = ConfigProvider.fromUnknown({})

  ["banana", 2],
  ["cherry", 3]
] as const

// Works with any iterable

console.log(MutableHashMap.get(fromMap, "x")) // Some(10)
```

name: Effect v4 Function: MutableHashMap.modifyAt
episode_body:
Title: MutableHashMap.modifyAt
Category: function
Module Path: effect/MutableHashMap
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: mutations

Description: Sets or removes the specified key in the MutableHashMap using an update function. The function receives the current value as an Option and returns an O. If the function returns Some, the key is set to that value. If the function returns None, the key is removed.

Signature: modifyAt: { <K, V>(key: K, f: (value: O.Option<V>) => O.Option<V>): (self: MutableHashMap<K, V>) => MutableHashMap<K, V> <K, V>(self: MutableHashMap<K, V>, key: K, f: (value: O.Option<V>) => O.Option<V>): MutableHashMap<K, V> } = dual< <K, V>(

Example:
```ts
import * as MutableHashMap from "effect/MutableHashMap"
import * as O from "effect/Option"

const map = MutableHashMap.make(["count", 5])

// Update existing key
MutableHashMap.modifyAt(
  map,
  "count",
  (option) => O.map(option, (n) => n * 2)
)
console.log(MutableHashMap.get(map, "count")) // Some(10)

// Add new key
MutableHashMap.modifyAt(
  map,
  "new",
  (option) => O.isNone(option) ? O.some(42) : option
)
console.log(MutableHashMap.get(map, "new")) // Some(42)

Title: MutableHashMap.clear
Category: function
Module Path: effect/MutableHashMap
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: mutations

Description: Removes all key-value pairs from the MutableHashMap, mutating the map in place. The map becomes empty after this operation.

Signature: clear = <K, V>(self: MutableHashMap<K, V>) => { self.backing.clear() self.buckets.clear() return self }

Example:
```ts
import * as MutableHashMap from "effect/MutableHashMap"

const map = MutableHashMap.make(
  ["key1", 42],
  ["key2", 100],
  ["key3", 200]
)

console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 3

// Clear all entries
MutableHashMap.clear(map)

console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 0
console.log(MutableHashMap.has(map, "key1")) // false

// Can still add new entries after clearing
MutableHashMap.set(map, "new", 999)
console.log(MutableHashMap.size(map)) // 1
```

name: Effect v4 Function: Function.dual
episode_body:
Title: Function.dual
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Function
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Creates a function that can be used in a data-last (aka `pipe`able) or data-first style. The first parameter to `dual` is either the arity of the uncurried function or a predicate that determines if the function is being used in a data-first or data-last style. Using the arity is the most common use case, but there are some cases where you may want to use a predicate. For example, if you have a function that takes an optional argument, you can use a predicate to determine if the function is bein

Signature: dual: { <DataLast extends (...args: Array<any>) => any, DataFirst extends (...args: Array<any>) => any>( arity: Parameters<DataFirst>["length"], body: DataFirst ): DataLast & DataFirst

Example:
```ts
import { dual, pipe } from "effect/Function"

// Using arity to determine data-first or data-last style
const sum = dual<
  (that: number) => (self: number) => number,
  (self: number, that: number) => number
>(2, (self, that) => self + that)

console.log(sum(2, 3)) // 5 (data-first)
console.log(pipe(2, sum(3))) // 5 (data-last)
```

Title: Function.compose

Description: Composes two functions, `ab` and `bc` into a single function that takes in an argument `a` of type `A` and returns a result of type `C`. The result is obtained by first applying the `ab` function to `a` and then applying the `bc` function to the result of `ab`.

Signature: compose: { <B, C>(bc: (b: B) => C): <A>(self: (a: A) => B) => (a: A) => C <A, B, C>(self: (a: A) => B, bc: (b: B) => C): (a: A) => C } = dual(2, <A, B, C>(ab: (a: A) => B, bc: (b: B) => C): (a: A) => C => (a) => bc(ab(a)))

Example:
```ts
import { compose } from "effect/Function"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const increment = (n: number) => n + 1
const square = (n: number) => n * n

assert.strictEqual(compose(increment, square)(2), 9)
```

name: Effect v4 Function: Function.pipe
episode_body:
Title: Function.pipe
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Function
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Pipes the value of an expression into a pipeline of functions. **Details** The `pipe` function is a utility that allows us to compose functions in a readable and sequential manner. It takes the output of one function and passes it as the input to the next function in the pipeline. This enables us to build complex transformations by chaining multiple functions together.

Signature: pipe<A>(a: A): A export function pipe<A, B = never>(a: A, ab: (a: A) => B): B export function pipe<A, B = never, C = never>( a: A, ab: (a: A) => B,

Example:
```ts
import { pipe } from "effect"

// Define simple arithmetic operations
const increment = (x: number) => x + 1
const double = (x: number) => x * 2
const subtractTen = (x: number) => x - 10

// Sequentially apply these operations using `pipe`
const result = pipe(5, increment, double, subtractTen)

console.log(result)
// Output: 2
```

Since: 2.0.0

Signature: flow<A extends ReadonlyArray<unknown>, B = never>( ab: (...a: A) => B ): (...a: A) => B export function flow<A extends ReadonlyArray<unknown>, B = never, C = never>( ab: (...a: A) => B,

Example:
```ts
import { flow } from "effect/Function"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

const len = (s: string): number => s.length
const double = (n: number): number => n * 2

const f = flow(len, double)

assert.strictEqual(f("aaa"), 6)
```

Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: The SK combinator, also known as the "S-K combinator" or "S-combinator", is a fundamental combinator in the lambda calculus and the SKI combinator calculus. This function is useful for discarding the first argument passed to it and returning the second argument.

Signature: SK = <A, B>(_: A, b: B): B => b

Example:
```ts
import { SK } from "effect/Function"
import * as assert from "node:assert"

assert.deepStrictEqual(SK(0, "hello"), "hello")
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: SchemaRepresentation.fromAST
episode_body:
Title: SchemaRepresentation.fromAST
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: Schema

Description: Schema codec for PrimitiveTree.

Signature: fromAST: (ast: AST.AST) => Document = InternalRepresentation.fromAST

Example:
```ts
import { Schema, SchemaRepresentation } from "effect"

const Person = Schema.Struct({
  name: Schema.String,
  age: Schema.Number
})

const doc = SchemaRepresentation.fromAST(Person.ast)
console.log(doc.representation._tag)
// "Objects"
```

Since: 4.0.0

Export Kind: const
Category Tag: uncategorized

Signature: fromASTs: (asts: readonly [AST.AST, ...Array<AST.AST>]) => MultiDocument = InternalRepresentation.fromASTs

name: Effect v4 Function: SchemaRepresentation.DocumentFromJson
episode_body:
Title: SchemaRepresentation.DocumentFromJson
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: uncategorized

Description: Schema codec that decodes a Document from JSON and encodes it back. - Use with `Schema.decodeUnknownSync` / `Schema.encodeSync` to serialize/deserialize documents.

Signature: DocumentFromJson: Schema.Codec<Document, unknown> = Schema.toCodecJson($Document)

Example:
```ts
import { Schema, SchemaRepresentation } from "effect"

const doc = SchemaRepresentation.fromAST(Schema.String.ast)
const json = Schema.encodeSync(SchemaRepresentation.DocumentFromJson)(doc)
const back = Schema.decodeUnknownSync(SchemaRepresentation.DocumentFromJson)(json)
```

Since: 4.0.0

Title: SchemaRepresentation.Reviver
Category: type
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: type
Category Tag: uncategorized

Description: A callback that handles Declaration nodes during reconstruction (toSchema) or code generation (toCodeDocument). - Return a value to handle the declaration. - Return `undefined` to fall back to default behavior (use `encodedSchema` for `toSchema`, or `generation` annotation for `toCodeDocument`). - `recur` processes child representations recursively.

Signature: Reviver<T> = (declaration: Declaration, recur: (representation: Representation) => T) => T | undefined

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: SchemaRepresentation.toSchemaDefaultReviver
episode_body:
Title: SchemaRepresentation.toSchemaDefaultReviver
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: uncategorized

Description: Default Reviver for toSchema that handles built-in Effect types (Option, Result, Redacted, Cause, Exit, ReadonlyMap, ReadonlySet, Date, Duration, URL, RegExp, etc.). - Pass as `options.reviver` to toSchema to reconstruct schemas that use these types. - Returns `undefined` for unrecognized declarations, causing fallback to `encodedSchema`.

Signature: toSchemaDefaultReviver: Reviver<Schema.Top> = (s, recur) => { const typeConstructor = s.annotations?.typeConstructor if (Predicate.isObject(typeConstructor) && typeof typeConstructor._tag === "string") { const typeParameters = s.typeParameters.map(recur) switch (typeConstructor._tag) {

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: SchemaRepresentation.toSchema
episode_body:
Title: SchemaRepresentation.toSchema
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: Runtime Generation

Description: Reconstructs a runtime Schema from a Document. - Use when you have a serialized or computed representation and need a working Schema for decoding/encoding. - Pass `options.reviver` (e.g. toSchemaDefaultReviver) to handle Declaration nodes for types like `Date`, `Option`, etc. - Without a reviver, declarations fall back to their `encodedSchema`. - Handles circular references via lazy `Schema.suspend`. - Throws if a `$ref` is not found in `document.references`.

Signature: toSchema<S extends Schema.Top = Schema.Top>(document: Document, options?: { readonly reviver?: Reviver<Schema.Top> | undefined }): S { type Slot = { // 0 = not started, 1 = building, 2 = done

Example:
```ts
import { Schema, SchemaRepresentation } from "effect"

const doc = SchemaRepresentation.fromAST(
  Schema.Struct({ name: Schema.String }).ast
)

const schema = SchemaRepresentation.toSchema(doc)
console.log(JSON.stringify(Schema.toJsonSchemaDocument(schema), null, 2))
```

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: SchemaRepresentation.toJsonSchemaDocument
episode_body:
Title: SchemaRepresentation.toJsonSchemaDocument
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: uncategorized

Description: Converts a Document to a Draft 2020-12 JSON Schema document. - Use to produce a standard JSON Schema from an Effect Schema representation. - Pure function; does not mutate the input.

Signature: toJsonSchemaDocument: ( document: Document, options?: Schema.ToJsonSchemaOptions ) => JsonSchema.Document<"draft-2020-12"> = InternalRepresentation.toJsonSchemaDocument

Example:
```ts
import { Schema, SchemaRepresentation } from "effect"

const doc = SchemaRepresentation.fromAST(Schema.String.ast)
const jsonSchema = SchemaRepresentation.toJsonSchemaDocument(doc)
console.log(jsonSchema.schema.type)
// "string"
```

Since: 4.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: SchemaRepresentation.toCodeDocument
episode_body:
Title: SchemaRepresentation.toCodeDocument
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SchemaRepresentation
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: Code Generation

Description: Generates TypeScript code strings from a MultiDocument. - Use to produce source code for Schema definitions (e.g. for codegen tools). - `options.reviver` can customize code generation for Declaration nodes. Return `undefined` to fall back to the default logic (which uses `generation` annotations or the encoded schema). - Performs topological sorting of references to emit non-recursive definitions before their dependents. - Produces sanitized JavaScript identifiers for `$ref` keys.

Signature: toCodeDocument(multiDocument: MultiDocument, options?: { /** * The reviver can return `undefined` to indicate that the generation should be generated by the default logic */ readonly reviver?: Reviver<Code> | undefined

Example:
```ts
import { Schema, SchemaRepresentation } from "effect"

const Person = Schema.Struct({
  name: Schema.String,
  age: Schema.Int
})

const multi = SchemaRepresentation.toMultiDocument(
  SchemaRepresentation.fromAST(Person.ast)
)
const codeDoc = SchemaRepresentation.toCodeDocument(multi)
console.log(codeDoc.codes[0].runtime)
// Schema.Struct({ ... })
```

Example:
```ts

```

name: Effect v4 Type: Equivalence.Equivalence
episode_body:
Title: Equivalence.Equivalence
Category: type
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: type
Category Tag: type class

Description: Utilities for defining equivalence relations - binary relations that determine when two values should be considered equivalent. Equivalence relations are used for comparing, deduplicating, and organizing data in collections and data structures. ## Mental model - **Equivalence relation**: A function `(a: A, b: A) => boolean` that returns `true` when values are equivalent - **Reflexive property**: Every value is equivalent to itself (`eq(a, a) === true`) - **Symmetric property**: If `a` is equival

Signature: Equivalence<in A> = (self: A, that: A) => boolean

Example:
```ts
import { Array, Equivalence } from "effect"

const caseInsensitive = Equivalence.make<string>((a, b) =>
  a.toLowerCase() === b.toLowerCase()
)

const strings = ["Hello", "world", "HELLO", "World"]
const deduplicated = Array.dedupeWith(strings, caseInsensitive)
console.log(deduplicated) // ["Hello", "world"]
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: Equivalence.make
episode_body:
Title: Equivalence.make
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: Creates a custom equivalence relation with an optimized reference equality check. When to use this: - When you need a custom equivalence that isn't just strict equality - When creating equivalences for complex types with custom comparison logic - When you want the performance benefit of reference equality optimization Behavior: - Does not mutate inputs - First checks reference equality (`===`) for performance; if values are identical, returns `true` without calling the function - Falls back to t

Signature: make = <A>(isEquivalent: (self: A, that: A) => boolean): Equivalence<A> => (self: A, that: A): boolean => self === that || isEquivalent(self, that)

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

const caseInsensitive = Equivalence.make<string>((a, b) =>
  a.toLowerCase() === b.toLowerCase()
)

console.log(caseInsensitive("Hello", "HELLO")) // true
console.log(caseInsensitive("foo", "bar")) // false

// Same reference optimization
const str = "test"
console.log(caseInsensitive(str, str)) // true (fast path)
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: Equivalence.strictEqual
episode_body:
Title: Equivalence.strictEqual
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: constructors

Description: Creates an equivalence relation that uses strict equality (`===`) to compare values. When to use this: - For primitive types (numbers, strings, booleans) where `===` is appropriate - When you need reference equality for objects (same object instance) - As a building block for more complex equivalences via mapInput or combine - When performance is critical and you don't need structural equality Behavior: - Does not mutate inputs - Uses JavaScript's strict equality operator (`===`) - For primitive

Signature: strictEqual: <A>() => Equivalence<A> = () => isStrictEquivalent

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

const strictEq = Equivalence.strictEqual<number>()

console.log(strictEq(1, 1)) // true
console.log(strictEq(1, 2)) // false
console.log(strictEq(NaN, NaN)) // false (NaN !== NaN)
```

Since: 4.0.0

Title: Equivalence.mapInput
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: mapping

Description: Transforms an equivalence relation by mapping the input values before comparison. When to use this: - When you need an equivalence for a complex type based on a single property - When you want to normalize values before comparison (e.g., case-insensitive strings) - When creating equivalences that focus on specific fields of objects - As a building block for creating equivalences via combine or combineAll Behavior: - Does not mutate inputs - Applies the transformation function to both values befo

Signature: mapInput: { <B, A>(f: (b: B) => A): (self: Equivalence<A>) => Equivalence<B> <A, B>(self: Equivalence<A>, f: (b: B) => A): Equivalence<B> } = dual( 2,

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

interface User {
  id: number
  name: string
  email: string
}

// Create equivalence based on user ID only
const userByIdEq = Equivalence.mapInput(
  Equivalence.strictEqual<number>(),
  (user: User) => user.id
)


console.log(userByIdEq(user1, user2)) // true (same ID)
console.log(userByIdEq(user1, user3)) // false (different ID)
```

Title: Equivalence.Tuple
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Creates an equivalence for tuples with heterogeneous element types. When to use this: - When comparing tuples with different types at each position - When you need different equivalence logic for each tuple element - When working with fixed-length tuples (not arrays) - Prefer over `Array` when you have a known tuple structure with different types Behavior: - Does not mutate inputs - Requires tuples to have the same length; different lengths are never equivalent - Applies each equivalence to the 

Signature: Tuple<const Elements extends ReadonlyArray<Equivalence<any>>>( elements: Elements ): Equivalence<{ readonly [I in keyof Elements]: [Elements[I]] extends [Equivalence<infer A>] ? A : never }> { return make((self, that) => { if (self.length !== that.length) {

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

const stringTupleEq = Equivalence.Tuple([
  Equivalence.strictEqual<string>(),
  Equivalence.strictEqual<string>(),
  Equivalence.strictEqual<string>()
])

const tuple1 = ["hello", "world", "test"] as const
const tuple2 = ["hello", "world", "test"] as const
const tuple3 = ["hello", "world", "different"] as const

console.log(stringTupleEq(tuple1, tuple2)) // true
console.log(stringTupleEq(tuple1, tuple3)) // false (different third element)
```

```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

const numberArrayEq = Equivalence.Array(Equivalence.strictEqual<number>())

console.log(numberArrayEq([1, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3])) // true

name: Effect v4 Function: Equivalence.Record
episode_body:
Title: Equivalence.Record
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Creates an equivalence for objects by comparing all properties using the same equivalence. When to use this: - When comparing objects with dynamic or unknown property names - When all property values should use the same equivalence logic - When working with record/dictionary types (key-value maps) - Prefer over `Struct` when you have variable properties or need to compare all properties uniformly Behavior: - Does not mutate inputs - Compares all properties present in both objects - Requires both

Signature: Record<A>(value: Equivalence<A>): Equivalence<Record<PropertyKey, A>> { return make((self, that) => { const selfKeys = Reflect.ownKeys(self) const thatKeys = Reflect.ownKeys(that)

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

const stringRecordEq = Equivalence.Record(Equivalence.strictEqual<string>())

const record1 = { a: "hello", b: "world" }
const record2 = { a: "hello", b: "world" }
const record3 = { a: "hello", b: "different" }
const record4 = { a: "hello" } // missing key 'b'

console.log(stringRecordEq(record1, record2)) // true
console.log(stringRecordEq(record1, record3)) // false
console.log(stringRecordEq(record1, record4)) // false (different keys)
```

name: Effect v4 Function: Equivalence.makeReducer
episode_body:
Title: Equivalence.makeReducer
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Equivalence
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: utilities

Description: Creates a `Reducer` for combining `Equivalence` instances, useful for aggregating equivalences in collections. When to use this: - When you need to combine multiple equivalences from a collection using reducer patterns - When implementing fold operations over collections of equivalences - When working with reducers that operate on equivalences Behavior: - Returns a reducer that combines equivalences using combine - Uses an equivalence that always returns `true` as the identity element (for empty

Example:
```ts
import { Equivalence } from "effect"

const reducer = Equivalence.makeReducer<number>()
const equivalences = [
  Equivalence.strictEqual<number>(),
  Equivalence.make<number>((a, b) => Math.abs(a - b) < 1)
]

const combined = reducer.combineAll(equivalences)
// Combined equivalence requires both conditions to be true
console.log(combined(1, 1)) // true (strict equal)
console.log(combined(1, 1.5)) // false (strict equal fails)
```

name: Effect v4 Function: SubscriptionRef.changes
episode_body:
Title: SubscriptionRef.changes
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SubscriptionRef
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: changes

Description: Creates a stream that emits the current value and all subsequent changes to the `SubscriptionRef`. The stream will first emit the current value, then emit all future changes as they occur.

Signature: changes = <A>(self: SubscriptionRef<A>): Stream.Stream<A> => Stream.fromPubSub(self.pubsub)

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, Stream, SubscriptionRef } from "effect"

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  const ref = yield* SubscriptionRef.make(0)

  const stream = SubscriptionRef.changes(ref)

  const fiber = yield* Stream.runForEach(
    stream,
    (value) => Effect.sync(() => console.log("Value:", value))
  ).pipe(Effect.forkScoped)

  yield* SubscriptionRef.set(ref, 1)
  yield* SubscriptionRef.set(ref, 2)
})
```

name: Effect v4 Function: SubscriptionRef.getUnsafe
episode_body:
Title: SubscriptionRef.getUnsafe
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SubscriptionRef
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: getters

Description: Unsafely retrieves the current value of the `SubscriptionRef`. This function directly accesses the underlying reference without any synchronization. It should only be used when you're certain there are no concurrent modifications.

Signature: getUnsafe = <A>(self: SubscriptionRef<A>): A => self.backing.ref.current

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, SubscriptionRef } from "effect"

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  const ref = yield* SubscriptionRef.make(42)

  const value = SubscriptionRef.getUnsafe(ref)
  console.log(value)
})
```

Module Path: effect/SubscriptionRef
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: updating

Description: Updates the value of the `SubscriptionRef` with the result of applying an effectful function and returns the new value, notifying subscribers of the change.

Signature: updateAndGetEffect: { <A, E, R>(update: (a: A) => Effect.Effect<A, E, R>): (self: SubscriptionRef<A>) => Effect.Effect<A, E, R> <A, E, R>(self: SubscriptionRef<A>, update: (a: A) => Effect.Effect<A, E, R>): Effect.Effect<A, E, R> } = dual(2, <A, E, R>( self: SubscriptionRef<A>,

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, SubscriptionRef } from "effect"

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  const ref = yield* SubscriptionRef.make(10)

  const newValue = yield* SubscriptionRef.updateAndGetEffect(
    ref,
    (n) => Effect.succeed(n + 5)
  )
  console.log("New value:", newValue)
})
```

name: Effect v4 Function: SubscriptionRef.updateSome

Title: SubscriptionRef.updateSome
Category: function
Module Path: effect/SubscriptionRef
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: updating

Description: Optionally updates the value of the `SubscriptionRef` with the result of applying a function that returns an `Option`, notifying subscribers only if the value changes.

Signature: updateSome: { <A>(update: (a: A) => O.Option<A>): (self: SubscriptionRef<A>) => Effect.Effect<void> <A>(self: SubscriptionRef<A>, update: (a: A) => O.Option<A>): Effect.Effect<void> } = dual(2, <A>( self: SubscriptionRef<A>,

Example:
```ts
import { Effect, Option, SubscriptionRef } from "effect"

const program = Effect.gen(function*() {
  const ref = yield* SubscriptionRef.make(10)

  yield* SubscriptionRef.updateSome(
    ref,
    (n) => n > 5 ? O.some(n * 2) : O.none()
  )

  const value = yield* SubscriptionRef.get(ref)
  console.log(value)
})
```

name: Effect v4 Type: Predicate.Predicate
episode_body:
Title: Predicate.Predicate
Category: type
Module Path: effect/Predicate
Export Kind: interface
Category Tag: models

Description: Predicate and Refinement helpers for runtime checks, filtering, and type narrowing. This module provides small, pure functions you can combine to decide whether a value matches a condition and, when using refinements, narrow TypeScript types. Mental model: - A `Predicate<A>` is just `(a: A) => boolean`. - A `Refinement<A, B>` is a predicate that narrows `A` to `B` when true. - Guards like `isString` are predicates/refinements for common runtime types. - Combinators like `and`/`or` build new pred

Signature: Predicate<in A> { (a: A): boolean }

Example:
```ts
import * as P from "effect/Predicate"

const isPositive = (n: number) => n > 0
const data = [2, -1, 3]

console.log(data.filter(isPositive))
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Type: Predicate.Refinement
episode_body:
Title: Predicate.Refinement
Category: type
Module Path: effect/Predicate
Export Kind: interface
Category Tag: models

Description: A predicate that also narrows the input type when it returns `true`. When to use: - You want a runtime check that refines `A` to `B` for TypeScript. - You want to compose multiple type guards with compose. - You need to guard `unknown` values safely. Behavior: - Pure function; does not mutate input. - Returns a type predicate (`a is B`). - Use with `if`/`filter` to narrow types.

Signature: Refinement<in A, out B extends A> { (a: A): a is B }

Example:
```ts
import { Predicate } from "effect"

const isString: Predicate.Refinement<unknown, string> = (u): u is string => typeof u === "string"

const data: unknown = "hello"
if (isString(data)) {
  console.log(data.toUpperCase())
}
```

Since: 2.0.0

name: Effect v4 Function: Predicate.mapInput
episode_body:
Title: Predicate.mapInput
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Predicate
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Transforms the input of a predicate using a mapping function. When to use: - You have a predicate on `A` and want one on `B` via `B -> A`. - You want to check derived values (lengths, projections, etc.). Behavior: - Pure; does not mutate input. - Returns a new predicate that applies `f` before `self`. - No short-circuit beyond what `self` does.

Signature: mapInput: { <B, A>(f: (b: B) => A): (self: Predicate<A>) => Predicate<B> <A, B>(self: Predicate<A>, f: (b: B) => A): Predicate<B> } = dual(2, <A, B>(self: Predicate<A>, f: (b: B) => A): Predicate<B> => (b) => self(f(b)))

Example:
```ts
import { Predicate } from "effect"

const isLongerThan2 = Predicate.mapInput((s: string) => s.length)(
  (n: number) => n > 2
)

console.log(isLongerThan2("hello"))
```

Description: Checks whether a readonly array has exactly `n` elements. When to use: - You need a runtime check for tuple length. - You want to narrow `ReadonlyArray<T>` to `TupleOf<N, T>`. Behavior: - Pure; does not mutate input. - Only checks length, not element types. - Returns a refinement on the array type.

Signature: isTupleOf: { <N extends number>(n: N): <T>(self: ReadonlyArray<T>) => self is TupleOf<N, T> <T, N extends number>(self: ReadonlyArray<T>, n: N): self is TupleOf<N, T> } = dual(2, <T, N extends number>(self: ReadonlyArray<T>, n: N): self is TupleOf<N, T> => self.length === n)

Example:
```ts
import { Predicate } from "effect"

const isPair = Predicate.isTupleOf(2)

console.log(isPair([1, 2]))
```

name: Effect v4 Function: Predicate.isTruthy
episode_body:
Title: Predicate.isTruthy
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Predicate
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: guards

Description: Checks whether a value is truthy. When to use: - You want a predicate that mirrors JavaScript truthiness. - You need to filter out falsy values like `0`, "", and `false`. Behavior: - Pure; does not mutate input. - Uses `!!input` under the hood. - Treats `0`, "", `false`, `null`, and `undefined` as false.

Signature: isTruthy(input: unknown): boolean { return !!input }

Example:
```ts
import { Predicate } from "effect"

const values = [0, 1, "", "ok", false]
const truthy = values.filter(Predicate.isTruthy)

console.log(truthy)
```

Description: Checks whether a value is an object or an array (non-null object). When to use: - You want to accept plain objects and arrays, but not `null`. Behavior: - Pure; does not mutate input. - Uses `typeof input === "object" && input !== null`. - Includes arrays.

Signature: isObjectOrArray(input: unknown): input is { [x: PropertyKey]: unknown } | Array<unknown> { return typeof input === "object" && input !== null }

name: Effect v4 Function: Predicate.isObject

Title: Predicate.isObject
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Predicate
Export Kind: function
Category Tag: guards

Description: Checks whether a value is a plain object (not an array, not `null`). When to use: - You need to accept objects but exclude arrays. Behavior: - Pure; does not mutate input. - Uses `typeof input === "object" && input !== null && !Array.isArray(input)`.

Signature: isObject(input: unknown): input is { [x: PropertyKey]: unknown } { return typeof input === "object" && input !== null && !Array.isArray(input) }

Example:
```ts

console.log(Predicate.isObject({ a: 1 }))
console.log(Predicate.isObject([1, 2]))
```

name: Effect v4 Function: Predicate.compose
episode_body:
Title: Predicate.compose
Category: function
Module Path: effect/Predicate
Export Kind: const
Category Tag: combinators

Description: Composes two predicates or refinements into one. When to use: - You want to chain two refinements for progressive narrowing. - You want a predicate that applies two checks in sequence. Behavior: - Pure; does not mutate input. - For refinements, the output type is narrowed by both. - Short-circuits on the first `false`.

Signature: compose: { <A, B extends A, C extends B>(bc: Refinement<B, C>): (ab: Refinement<A, B>) => Refinement<A, C> <A, B extends A>(bc: Predicate<NoInfer<B>>): (ab: Refinement<A, B>) => Refinement<A, B> <A, B extends A, C extends B>(ab: Refinement<A, B>, bc: Refinement<B, C>): Refinement<A, C> <A, B extends

Example:
```ts
import { Predicate } from "effect"

const isNumber: Predicate.Refinement<unknown, number> = (u): u is number => typeof u === "number"
const isInteger: Predicate.Refinement<number, number> = (n): n is number => Number.isInteger(n)

const isIntegerNumber = Predicate.compose(isNumber, isInteger)

console.log(isIntegerNumber(1))
```
